title: ACAS Code of Practice on Settlement Agreements
topicLabel: Negotiating exit agreements and protected conversations
issuedUnder: Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992 s.199
chapters: Statutory Code of Practice · Issued under TULRCA 1992 s.199 · In effect 29 July 2013 · Replaced previous 'Compromise Agreements' guidance following Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 renaming
desc: The ACAS Code of Practice on Settlement Agreements provides guidance on the use of settlement agreements (formerly compromise agreements) in employment disputes. It covers the proper conduct of pre-termination negotiations under ERA 1996 s.111A (introduced by ERRA 2013), the requirements for a valid settlement agreement under ERA 1996 s.203(3), and the role of independent legal advice. The Code establishes that an employer may offer a settlement agreement where there is a workplace problem, even before any formal procedure has begun, and that evidence of pre-termination negotiations is inadmissible in unfair dismissal proceedings unless there has been improper behaviour. Employment tribunals may adjust awards by up to 25% for unreasonable failure to comply with the Code.
legalStatus: Statutory Code of Practice. Employment tribunals will take the Code into account when considering relevant cases. The 25% adjustment under TULRCA 1992 s.207A applies.

sections.title: Purpose and legal framework for settlement agreements
sections.summary: Settlement agreements (formerly compromise agreements, renamed by ERRA 2013) are legally binding agreements between an employer and employee under which the employee agrees to settle their claims in return for a payment or other consideration. For a settlement agreement to be valid under ERA 1996 s.203(3): it must be in writing, it must relate to particular proceedings or particular complaints, the employee must have received advice from a relevant independent adviser on the terms and effect of the agreement, the adviser must be identified in the agreement, the adviser must have insurance covering the risk of their advice, and the agreement must state that the statutory conditions are satisfied.
sections.text:
A settlement agreement is a legally binding agreement between an employer and an employee (or former employee) under which the employee agrees to settle claims they may have in return for a payment or some other consideration. For the agreement to be valid it must comply with the requirements set out in section 203(3) of the Employment Rights Act 1996.

The Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 renamed compromise agreements as settlement agreements and introduced section 111A of the Employment Rights Act 1996, which provides that evidence of pre-termination negotiations is inadmissible in ordinary unfair dismissal proceedings.

The Code provides guidance on the proper use of settlement agreements and pre-termination negotiations. A failure to follow the Code does not in itself create liability, but tribunals may adjust awards by up to 25% for unreasonable failure to comply.

versions.description: Code came into effect alongside ERA 1996 s.111A (pre-termination negotiations). ERRA 2013 renamed compromise agreements as settlement agreements.

leadingCases.name: Faithorn Farrell Timms LLP v Bailey [2016] UKEAT/0025/16
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT considered the scope of ERA 1996 s.111A and held that 'improper behaviour' which lifts the inadmissibility protection is not limited to the list in the ACAS Code. The tribunal must consider whether the conduct was improper in all the circumstances. Undue pressure or threats can constitute improper behaviour.
leadingCases.significance: Key authority on the scope of 'improper behaviour' that removes s.111A inadmissibility protection

leadingCases.name: Portnykh v Nomura International plc [2014] IRLR 251
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that section 111A does not apply to discrimination claims. The inadmissibility protection is limited to ordinary unfair dismissal proceedings. Pre-termination negotiations may be admissible in discrimination or whistleblowing claims even if they would be inadmissible in unfair dismissal proceedings.
leadingCases.significance: Established that s.111A inadmissibility does not extend to discrimination claims

refs: ERA 1996 s.111A (pre-termination negotiations — inadmissibility in unfair dismissal)
ERA 1996 s.203(3) (validity requirements for settlement agreements)
Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 s.14 (renaming and s.111A introduction)
TULRCA 1992 s.207A (25% adjustment for Code non-compliance)

sections.title: Initiating and conducting pre-termination negotiations
sections.summary: An employer may initiate a conversation with an employee where there is a workplace problem with a view to agreeing a settlement. This may happen before any formal procedure has started. The conversation should be conducted in a manner that is not improper. The employee should be given a reasonable period to consider the offer (normally at least 10 calendar days unless the parties agree otherwise). The employee should be advised that they have the right to seek independent advice and should be allowed to be accompanied at any meeting to discuss the settlement.
sections.text:
An employer may initiate a conversation with an employee with a view to agreeing termination of employment on agreed terms. Section 111A of the Employment Rights Act 1996 provides that evidence of pre-termination negotiations is inadmissible in ordinary unfair dismissal proceedings unless there has been improper behaviour.

Improper behaviour includes (but is not limited to): harassment, bullying, intimidation, assault or threat of assault, putting undue pressure on the employee (such as not giving them reasonable time to consider the offer), saying before any form of disciplinary process has begun that if a settlement is not agreed the employee will be dismissed, or an employer not following the ACAS Code of Practice on settlement agreements.

The employee should be given a reasonable period to consider the offer. What is reasonable will depend on the circumstances. As a general guide, a minimum of 10 calendar days should be allowed.

The employee should be informed of their right to seek independent advice. The employer should allow the employee to be accompanied at any meeting to discuss the settlement agreement by a work colleague or trade union representative.

leadingCases.name: Basra v BJSS Ltd [2018] UKEAT/0090/17
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT considered what constitutes 'improper behaviour' in the context of pre-termination negotiations. Tying the offer to a very short deadline or putting pressure on the employee can amount to improper behaviour, removing the s.111A protection.
leadingCases.significance: Illustrates the boundary between legitimate negotiation and improper pressure in settlement discussions

refs: Without Prejudice rule (common law — s.111A operates independently of without prejudice privilege)

sections.title: Requirements for a valid settlement agreement
sections.summary: For a settlement agreement to be valid and to prevent the employee from bringing the claims covered by it, the agreement must satisfy the requirements of ERA 1996 s.203(3). These are: the agreement must be in writing; it must relate to the particular complaint or proceedings; the employee must have received advice from a relevant independent adviser (a qualified lawyer, a certified trade union official, or a certified advice centre worker) as to the terms and effect of the proposed agreement and its effect on the employee's ability to pursue those rights before an employment tribunal; the adviser must be identified in the agreement; the adviser must have a current contract of insurance or professional indemnity insurance covering the risk of a claim by the employee in respect of loss arising from the advice; and the agreement must state that the statutory conditions regulating settlement agreements are satisfied.

leadingCases.name: Hinton v University of East London [2005] ICR 1260
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that a settlement agreement must identify with sufficient precision the claims being compromised. A general waiver of 'all claims' without specifying the particular complaints or proceedings may not satisfy the statutory requirements.
leadingCases.significance: Key authority on the specificity required in settlement agreements — particular claims must be identified

refs: ERA 1996 s.203(3) (conditions for valid settlement agreement)
ERA 1996 s.203(3A) (definition of relevant independent adviser)
EqA 2010 s.147 (settlement of equality claims — parallel requirements)

pendingAmendments: No ERA 2025 amendments to settlement agreement framework
s.111A inadmissibility limited to ordinary unfair dismissal — discrimination claims unaffected

parliamentaryOrigin.issuedUnder: Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992 s.199
parliamentaryOrigin.statutoryBasis: Statutory Code of Practice — approved by resolution of both Houses of Parliament
parliamentaryOrigin.legalEffect: s.207A 25% adjustment applies. Issued alongside ERA 1996 s.111A (pre-termination negotiations) introduced by ERRA 2013. The Code established the framework for pre-termination negotiations and the inadmissibility of settlement discussions in unfair dismissal proceedings.
parliamentaryOrigin.note: Code of Practice 4 governs the proper conduct of settlement agreements and pre-termination negotiations. The 10-day minimum consideration period, the requirement for independent legal advice, and the definition of 'improper behaviour' are all established by the Code.

precedingDocuments.title: Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 s.14
precedingDocuments.significance: Introduced ERA 1996 s.111A (pre-termination negotiations), renamed compromise agreements as settlement agreements, and provided the policy framework for the Code.

leadingCases.name: Faithorn Farrell Timms v Bailey [2016] UKEAT/0025/16
leadingCases.heldText: 'Improper behaviour' lifting s.111A protection is not limited to the Code's list — tribunal must consider all circumstances.
leadingCases.significance: Scope of improper behaviour beyond the Code's examples.

leadingCases.name: Portnykh v Nomura International [2014] IRLR 251
leadingCases.heldText: s.111A inadmissibility does not extend to discrimination claims — only ordinary unfair dismissal.
leadingCases.significance: Critical limitation — s.111A does not cover discrimination.

leadingCases.name: Hinton v University of East London [2005] ICR 1260
leadingCases.heldText: Settlement agreements must identify particular claims being compromised with sufficient precision.
leadingCases.significance: Specificity requirement for valid settlement agreements.
